input.instructions:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-S4-ahc030-polyomino-mining
Title: S4. AHC030 / Polyomino Mining

Problem:
Given known polyomino oil-field shapes but unknown placements, recover all oil-containing cells with as little probing cost as possible using drilling, noisy aggregate queries, and final guesses.

Grading target:
Lower probing cost is better.
Contest score is derived from absolute and relative cost.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

variables.selected_prompt_text:
Write a tentative answer and ask one reviewer child to act as a Technical Co-Author. The reviewer's sole task is to identify every high-level concept in the draft (e.g., 'Bayesian update', 'EIG', 'search strategy') and demand the explicit mathematical formulation, specific data structure, or step-by-step pseudocode required to implement it. The reviewer must not merely critique the strategy, but must actively provide the missing technical details to transform the draft into a complete, implementable technical specification.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "review",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "draft_response": "plain-text draft answer",
  "message_for_reviewer": "plain-text review request for the child"
}
variables.child_selected_prompt_text:
Solve the question directly.
Keep the entire JSON compact.
Keep decision_notes extremely short. Prefer 3 to 8 words.
Do not narrate your reasoning outside the response field.
In the response field, use exactly two short lines encoded with \n:
FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>
Keep the justification to 12 words or fewer.
Use plain text only inside the response field. Do not use markdown formatting, code fences, or math-style backslashes such as \equiv.
Do not add any extra text before or after the JSON object.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "execute_locally",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "response": "FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>\nJUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>",
  "result_type": "summary"
}

orchestration.decision_notes: The child result provides a complete mathematical and algorithmic framework for solving the problem using Bayesian active learning, which is the optimal approach for minimizing probing costs in uncertain environments.

    "summary": "FINAL_ANSWER: Implement a Bayesian active learning framework: 1. Spatial Prior: Initialize P(c) = (1/N) * sum_{i,p} I(c in polyomino_i at position p), where N is total valid placements. 2. Likelihood Model: For aggregate query Q on set S, use P(Q|S) = Binomial(Q; |S_oil|, p_noise), where S_oil is the true oil count in S. 3. EIG Selection: Select probe S to maximize EIG(S) = H(P) - E[H(P|Q)], where H(P) = -sum P(c)logP(c). 4. Refinement: Maintain a set of consistent placements M = {m | m matches all drilling/aggregate results}. 5. Final Guess: Select cells C = {c | sum_{m in M} I(c in m) / |M| > threshold}. \nJUSTIFICATION: Provides explicit mathematical formulations and implementation steps for the Bayesian strategy."
  }

Question:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-S4-ahc030-polyomino-mining
Title: S4. AHC030 / Polyomino Mining

Problem:
Given known polyomino oil-field shapes but unknown placements, recover all oil-containing cells with as little probing cost as possible using drilling, noisy aggregate queries, and final guesses.

Grading target:
Lower probing cost is better.
Contest score is derived from absolute and relative cost.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

results.content: FINAL_ANSWER: Implement a Bayesian active learning framework: 1. Spatial Prior: Initialize P(c) = 1/N * sum_{i,p} I(c in polyomino_i at position p), where N is the total number of valid placements. 2. Likelihood Model: For an aggregate query Q on a set of cells S, use P(Q|S) = Binomial(Q; |S_oil|, p_noise), where S_oil is the true number of oil cells in S. 3. EIG Selection: Select the next probe S to maximize Expected Information Gain (EIG): EIG(S) = H(P) - E[H(P|Q)], where H(P) = -sum P(c)log P(c) is the current entropy. 4. Refinement: Maintain a set of consistent placements M = {m | m matches all drilling and aggregate query results}. 5. Final Guess: After the budget is exhausted, select cells C = {c | (sum_{m in M} I(c in m))/|M| > threshold}. JUSTIFICATION: This strategy minimizes probing cost by using information theory (Entropy/EIG) to select queries that most significantly reduce uncertainty about the polyomino placements, while the Bayesian update handles the noise in aggregate queries.
